past_medical_history: Hypertension, well controlled on current medications for 12 years
Type 2 diabetes mellitus, well controlled with oral agents, diagnosed 8 years ago
Osteoarthritis of bilateral knees and lumbar spine
Right lower-extremity chronic venous stasis ulcer, managed with wound care

medications.indication: Neck pain (recently prescribed from prior ED visit)
Muscle spasm (recently prescribed from prior ED visit)

family_history: Father died of stroke at age 74
Mother has type 2 diabetes and hypertension
No family history of neurological disease or immunodeficiency

social_history.smoking: Former smoker, quit 15 years ago, 10 pack-year history
social_history.living_situation: Lives with wife in a single-story home
social_history.exercise: Limited by osteoarthritis, walks 20 minutes daily

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, and time. Speech is clear and fluent. Follows commands appropriately. Appears mildly uncomfortable but cooperative. GCS 15 (E4 V5 M6). No confusion or disorientation at this time.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils pinpoint bilaterally with sluggish light reactivity. No photophobia elicited on direct testing. Extraocular movements intact without nystagmus. Facial sensation intact to light touch bilaterally. Face symmetric. Visual fields full to confrontation. Hearing grossly intact bilaterally. Gag reflex intact. Tongue midline. No ptosis.
neurological_exam.motor: Full strength 5/5 in bilateral upper and lower extremities. No pronator drift. Tone normal throughout. No involuntary movements noted aside from involuntary left rotation of the neck.
neurological_exam.sensory: Intact to light touch, pinprick, and proprioception in bilateral upper and lower extremities. No dermatomal sensory loss identified.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally, triceps 2+ bilaterally, brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally. Patellar 2+ bilaterally, Achilles 1+ bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing intact bilaterally without dysmetria. Heel-to-shin testing intact bilaterally. No truncal ataxia.
neurological_exam.gait: Ambulates independently with mildly antalgic gait favoring the right lower extremity, attributed to chronic knee osteoarthritis. Tandem gait not tested due to right lower-extremity pain. Romberg negative.
neurological_exam.additional: Head is held in involuntary left rotation consistent with torticollis. There is left paraspinal cervical tenderness. No midline vertebral tenderness. Range of motion of the cervical spine is significantly decreased in all planes due to pain and involuntary muscle contraction. Kernig sign not formally tested on initial presentation. Brudzinski sign not formally tested on initial presentation. No petechial rash on skin examination. A chronic venous stasis ulcer is present on the right lower extremity with clean dressing in place.

patient.chief_complaint: Persistent neck pain and stiffness for 15 days with near-syncopal episode today
patient.history_present_illness: Mr. Gerald Novak is a 59-year-old right-handed retired warehouse manager who presents to the emergency department after a near-syncopal episode earlier today. His symptoms began 15 days ago with the insidious onset of neck stiffness, which he initially attributed to sleeping in an awkward position. He visited his primary care physician five days into his symptoms and was prescribed methocarbamol for presumed musculoskeletal neck pain. Despite the muscle relaxant, his symptoms did not improve. Two days later, he presented to this emergency department reporting involuntary contractions of the neck muscles causing his head to be held in left rotation. At that initial ED visit, he denied headache, photophobia, fever, nausea, vomiting, upper-extremity weakness or numbness, stridor, dysphagia, drooling, odynophagia, or respiratory symptoms. He had no history of trauma. He was afebrile with normal vital signs, and imaging of the head and cervical spine was obtained. He reported significant pain relief after receiving intramuscular ketorolac and was discharged with Tylenol #3 and instructions for primary care follow-up. Four days later he returned to the ED with persistent neck pain. He remained afebrile, his examination was unchanged, and he was treated with ketorolac and discharged with acetaminophen/hydrocodone, cyclobenzaprine, and ibuprofen. Today, six days after his second ED visit, he experienced a near-syncopal episode upon standing from a seated position. He reports feeling dizzy and lightheaded, which he attributes to the cyclobenzaprine. He again denies headache, fever, chills, nausea, or vomiting. His wife reports that he has seemed more fatigued over the past several days and has had a poor appetite. She notes that his neck has remained persistently stiff and twisted despite multiple medications. On arrival to the ED, he is tachycardic at 120 beats per minute but afebrile.

findings.location: Diffuse, bilateral cerebral convexities and along the basal cisterns

signal_characteristics.T1_post_contrast: Diffuse leptomeningeal enhancement along bilateral cerebral convexities and basal cisterns, most prominent along the superior sagittal sinus and Sylvian fissures
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Hyperintense signal within bilateral cerebral sulci consistent with proteinaceous or inflammatory material in the subarachnoid space
signal_characteristics.DWI: No restricted diffusion within the brain parenchyma. Subtle increased signal in several cortical sulci bilaterally, suggesting purulent material
signal_characteristics.T2: Hyperintense signal within the sulci bilaterally

findings.mass_effect: No midline shift. No uncal herniation. Mild diffuse cerebral edema with subtle sulcal effacement.
findings.borders: Diffuse and bilateral without focal parenchymal lesion

signal_characteristics.T2: Mild disc desiccation at C4-C5 and C5-C6 with mild posterior disc bulging. No significant spinal canal stenosis or cord compression.

additional_observations: No subdural empyema or epidural abscess identified
No evidence of cerebritis or brain abscess formation
No hydrocephalus; ventricular system is normal in size and configuration
Mild generalized cerebral volume loss appropriate for age
Cervical degenerative changes are present but do not explain the clinical presentation
mri.impression: Mild diffuse cerebral edema without herniation. Degenerative cervical spine changes at C4-C5 and C5-C6 without significant stenosis or cord compression, unlikely to account for the torticollis presentation. No abscess, empyema, or vascular complication identified. Clinical correlation recommended.

ecg.interpretation: Sinus tachycardia at 120 bpm. Otherwise normal ECG. No acute ischemic changes. The tachycardia is likely a physiologic response to an underlying systemic process.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: WBC 34.5 x10^3/mm3 (H), Neutrophils 92 % (H), Bands 6 % (H), Lymphocytes 4 % (L), Sodium 134 mEq/L (L), Bicarbonate 20 mEq/L (L), BUN 22 mg/dL (H), Glucose 186 mg/dL (H), CRP 185 mg/L (H), Procalcitonin 5.4 ng/mL (H), ESR 72 mm/hr (H), Lactate 2.8 mmol/L (H), HbA1c 6.4 % (H).

special_tests.Gram_stain: No organisms seen on initial Gram stain
special_tests.Bacterial_antigen_panel: Positive for Group B Streptococcus (Streptococcus agalactiae) antigen
special_tests.CSF_lactate: 9.6 mmol/L (markedly elevated; normal <3.5 mmol/L; >4 strongly suggests bacterial meningitis)

csf.interpretation: Opening pressure: 28 cmH2O. WBC: 1310 cells/microL (markedly elevated; normal <5) (94% PMN/2% lymph). Protein: 248 mg/dL (markedly elevated; normal 15-45 mg/dL). Glucose: 18 mg/dL (very low; normal 40-70 mg/dL) (ratio 0.10 (CSF:serum ratio; markedly low, normal >0.6)). Gram_stain: No organisms seen on initial Gram stain. Bacterial_antigen_panel: Positive for Group B Streptococcus (Streptococcus agalactiae) antigen. CSF_lactate: 9.6 mmol/L (markedly elevated; normal <3.5 mmol/L; >4 strongly suggests bacterial meningitis).

output.appearance: Slightly hazy, improved from initial turbid specimen

special_tests.CSF_culture: No growth at 48 hours (sterilized by antibiotics)

output.interpretation: Opening pressure: 19 cmH2O. WBC: 215 cells/microL (decreased from 1310) (55% PMN/35% lymph). Protein: 142 mg/dL (decreased from 248 mg/dL, still elevated). Glucose: 38 mg/dL (improved from 18 mg/dL, approaching normal) (ratio 0.30 (improved from 0.10, still below normal >0.6)). Gram_stain: No organisms seen. CSF_culture: No growth at 48 hours (sterilized by antibiotics). CSF_lactate: 4.2 mmol/L (decreased from 9.6, still mildly elevated).

signal_characteristics.CT: Normal gray-white matter differentiation. No acute intracranial hemorrhage. Normal aerated sinuses.

findings.mass_effect: No midline shift. No mass effect. Basal cisterns patent.

signal_characteristics.CT: Degenerative changes with mild disc space narrowing and osteophyte formation at C4-C5 and C5-C6. No fracture. Prevertebral soft tissues appear intact. Some straightening of the cervical lordosis. No significant compression.

additional_observations: LP is safe to proceed; no contraindication to lumbar puncture identified
output.impression: Normal CT head without acute intracranial pathology. No contraindication to lumbar puncture. Degenerative cervical spine changes without fracture or significant compression. Straightening of cervical lordosis may reflect muscle spasm. Clinical correlation recommended.

Blood Cultures.value: Positive at 18 hours: Gram-positive cocci in chains
Positive at 20 hours: Gram-positive cocci in chains

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Blood Culture (Aerobic) - Bottle 1 Positive at 18 hours: Gram-positive cocci in chains  (H), Blood Culture (Aerobic) - Bottle 2 Positive at 20 hours: Gram-positive cocci in chains  (H).

Audiometry.test: High Frequency Average (4-8 kHz) - Right Ear
High Frequency Average (4-8 kHz) - Left Ear

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: High Frequency Average (4-8 kHz) - Right Ear 28 dB HL dB HL (H).

output.query: Group B Streptococcus meningitis adults non-pregnant risk factors treatment

results.title: Invasive Group B Streptococcal Disease in Nonpregnant Adults
results.authors: Farley MM, Harvey RC, Stull T, et al.
results.key_finding: GBS disease in nonpregnant adults is associated with diabetes mellitus, liver disease, malignancy, and advanced age. Meningitis accounts for approximately 4% of invasive GBS disease in adults.

results.title: Group B Streptococcal Meningitis in Adults: A Systematic Review
results.authors: van Kassel MN, Bijlsma MW, Brouwer MC, et al.
results.key_finding: Adult GBS meningitis has a case fatality rate of 15-27%. Diabetes mellitus is the most common comorbidity. Treatment with high-dose penicillin G or ampicillin for 14-21 days is recommended. Some experts add gentamicin for synergy during the first 5-7 days.

results.title: Practice Guidelines for the Management of Bacterial Meningitis
results.authors: Tunkel AR, Hartman BJ, Kaplan SL, et al.
results.key_finding: For GBS meningitis, recommended treatment is penicillin G 4 million units IV every 4 hours or ampicillin 2g IV every 4 hours for 14-21 days. Adjunctive dexamethasone evidence is strongest for pneumococcal meningitis; benefit in GBS meningitis is less established.

output.summary: Group B Streptococcus (Streptococcus agalactiae) meningitis in nonpregnant adults is uncommon but carries significant mortality (15-27%). Risk factors include diabetes mellitus, advanced age, liver disease, and immunosuppression. Treatment consists of high-dose penicillin G (4 million units IV q4h) or ampicillin (2g IV q4h) for 14-21 days, with optional addition of gentamicin for synergy during the first 5-7 days. The role of adjunctive dexamethasone is less established for GBS meningitis compared to pneumococcal meningitis. The chronic lower-extremity ulcer in this patient may have served as a portal of entry for GBS bacteremia.

output.proposed: Vancomycin 15-20 mg/kg IV plus Ceftriaxone 2g IV q12h plus Ampicillin 2g IV q4h (empiric meningitis coverage including Listeria)
interactions: Vancomycin + Metformin: Monitor renal function closely; both can affect renal clearance
Vancomycin: Requires therapeutic drug monitoring (trough levels 15-20 mcg/mL for meningitis)
warnings: Vancomycin requires dose adjustment based on renal function and trough levels for meningitis dosing
Given the patient's age (>50) and diabetes, ampicillin should be included in empiric regimen for Listeria coverage
Monitor renal function daily while on vancomycin
Cyclobenzaprine and hydrocodone should be discontinued to accurately assess mental status
alternatives: Meropenem 2g IV q8h can be substituted for ceftriaxone plus ampicillin if beta-lactam allergy is present (not the case here)

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Acute bacterial meningitis due to Streptococcus agalactiae (Group B Streptococcus)

differential.diagnosis: Cervical spine pathology (degenerative disc disease, fracture)
differential.key_distinguishing: CT and MRI of the cervical spine show only degenerative changes without fracture or significant compression. Degenerative changes are common in this age group and do not explain the marked leukocytosis or the progressive clinical course. Torticollis from cervical spine pathology would not be associated with WBC 34,500.

Viral meningitis presents with lymphocytic pleocytosis, normal or mildly elevated protein, and normal glucose. This patient has neutrophilic pleocytosis, markedly elevated protein, very low glucose, and positive GBS antigen.
No pharyngeal erythema, tonsillar inflammation, dysphagia, or odynophagia on examination. CT neck showed intact prevertebral soft tissues without abscess collection.

SAH presents with sudden thunderclap headache and xanthochromia. This patient has a subacute progressive course with neck stiffness rather than acute headache. CT head was normal. CSF shows neutrophilic pleocytosis rather than xanthochromia.

TB meningitis typically has a more gradual course over weeks, lymphocytic pleocytosis, basilar enhancement, and risk factors for TB exposure. This patient has neutrophilic pleocytosis and positive GBS antigen.

optimal_actions.action: Recognize that persistent neck stiffness with torticollis in the setting of markedly elevated WBC (34,500) and tachycardia in an afebrile patient should raise suspicion for occult meningitis, even without classic fever or headache
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Torticollis as an atypical presentation of meningeal irritation; WBC 34,500 with 92% neutrophils is incompatible with simple musculoskeletal neck pain

optimal_actions.action: Obtain laboratory studies including CBC with differential, BMP, inflammatory markers (CRP, procalcitonin, lactate), and blood cultures
optimal_actions.expected_finding: WBC 34,500 with 92% neutrophils, CRP 185, procalcitonin 5.4, elevated lactate indicating severe bacterial infection

optimal_actions.action: Obtain CT head to rule out contraindications to lumbar puncture
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal CT head without mass lesion or herniation; safe to proceed with LP

optimal_actions.action: Administer empiric IV antibiotics immediately (vancomycin plus ceftriaxone; add ampicillin for Listeria coverage given age >50 and diabetes)
Perform lumbar puncture with opening pressure measurement and send CSF for cell count, protein, glucose, Gram stain, culture, bacterial antigens, and lactate
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Turbid CSF, elevated opening pressure, WBC 1310 (94% neutrophils), protein 248, glucose 18, GBS antigen positive

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI with and without contrast to evaluate extent of meningeal involvement and rule out complications
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Diffuse leptomeningeal enhancement, sulcal FLAIR hyperintensity consistent with meningitis

optimal_actions.action: Narrow antibiotic therapy to high-dose penicillin G or ampicillin once GBS is confirmed and susceptibilities are available
Discontinue cyclobenzaprine, hydrocodone, and methocarbamol to avoid masking neurological symptoms and accurately assess mental status
Monitor and manage hyponatremia from potential SIADH with serial sodium levels and fluid management
Evaluate the right lower-extremity venous stasis ulcer as a potential portal of entry for GBS bacteremia
Search medical literature on GBS meningitis in nonpregnant adults for treatment duration and adjunctive therapy guidance
optimal_actions.expected_finding: GBS meningitis requires 14-21 days of treatment; consider synergistic gentamicin for first 5-7 days

optimal_actions.action: Assess hearing with audiometry prior to discharge
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Audiometry to screen for sensorineural hearing loss as a complication of bacterial meningitis

critical_actions: Recognize that torticollis with severe leukocytosis (WBC 34,500) indicates a serious infection, not simple musculoskeletal pathology
Obtain blood cultures before antibiotic administration
Administer empiric IV antibiotics (vancomycin plus ceftriaxone, with ampicillin for Listeria coverage given age >50) immediately upon suspicion of meningitis
Perform lumbar puncture with CSF analysis including cell count, protein, glucose, Gram stain, culture, and bacterial antigens
Obtain CT head before LP to rule out contraindications
Admit to ICU or monitored setting for close neurological and hemodynamic monitoring
contraindicated_actions: Discharging the patient a third time with oral analgesics and muscle relaxants without investigating the cause of marked leukocytosis
Delaying antibiotic administration to wait for CT, LP, or culture results
Attributing the tachycardia solely to cyclobenzaprine without considering sepsis
Using fluoroquinolone monotherapy for suspected bacterial meningitis
Performing LP without first obtaining CT in a patient with altered sensorium or focal deficits
Withholding lumbar puncture because the patient is afebrile and lacks classic meningeal signs
key_reasoning_points: Meningeal irritation can present with torticollis or opisthotonus rather than classic nuchal rigidity, and this atypical presentation can lead to repeated misdiagnosis as musculoskeletal neck pain
The absence of fever does not rule out bacterial meningitis; up to 5-20% of adult bacterial meningitis cases may be afebrile at presentation, particularly in elderly or immunocompromised patients

The dramatically elevated WBC (34,500 with 92% neutrophils) is a critical clue that the neck stiffness has an infectious rather than musculoskeletal etiology, and should have prompted further workup at earlier ED visits

CSF findings of neutrophilic pleocytosis (WBC 1310), markedly elevated protein (248 mg/dL), very low glucose (CSF:serum ratio 0.10), and positive GBS antigen are diagnostic for bacterial meningitis despite negative Gram stain
Group B Streptococcus meningitis in nonpregnant adults is associated with diabetes mellitus and may have a portal of entry through skin breaks such as the chronic lower-extremity ulcer

A negative Gram stain does not exclude bacterial meningitis; Gram stain sensitivity ranges from 60-90% depending on organism and bacterial load, making bacterial antigen testing and PCR essential complementary studies

This case illustrates a diagnostic pitfall: three ED visits over 15 days with progressive symptoms before the correct diagnosis was made, emphasizing the importance of reconsidering the differential when initial treatment fails

metadata.difficulty_description: Straightforward - despite the atypical torticollis presentation, the markedly elevated WBC on admission labs and classic CSF findings make the diagnosis clear once appropriate workup is initiated. The case is straightforward once meningitis is considered, though the clinical presentation initially mimics musculoskeletal pathology.
